segments.text: Take a Chinese [music] vacuum cleaner clawed and a PlayStation controller. And apparently you can see inside 7,000 homes in 24 countries. [music]
Yep, this is exactly what happened and by accident. So Sammy, a software engineer, bought this DJI robot vacuum and all he wanted to do was control it
with his PlayStation controller. So he uses claw to figure out how to connect to his vacuum through DJI servers and it works. But then he notices that he's
able to control 7,000 other vacuums. He was able to access live camera recordings, microphones, and locations for people's homes worldwide. It's kind
of like you check into a hotel, but then you realize your key opens every single room on every single floor, but nobody
at the hotel notices. Similarly, DTS servers never checked if Sammi's key was only for his vacuum. They just saw that
it was a valid key and said, "Come on in into [music] every single room or every single vacuum." But the craziest part is he didn't break a single rule. In fact,
he reported it to DJI and DJI fixed [music] this bug. He's an outlier,
though. There are many, many bad actors out there. There are hackers who have beat security cameras, smart locks, baby monitors, all sorts of smart devices.
So, if you're somebody who has a smart device at home, please change the default passwords and check what permissions it has. [music] If it does not need camera and microphone access,
disable it right now and share the story with everybody so people realize how easy it is to hack into smart devices.
